200.prompt: What more specific detail from "Technologies That Seemed Close but Never Arrived" should a alpaca remember after the obvious fact?
200.correctAnswer: At the entry level, this teaches students to question predictions instead of simply believing them. At the section level, it gives the introduction a bridge into futurism. Key examples to keep available: Nuclear-Powered Cars; Food Pills; 3D Television; Why Some Futures Stay “Almost There”.
wrongAnswers: The entry mainly proves that every predicted technology eventually succeeds if people wait long enough.
The key lesson is that nuclear cars, food pills, and 3D television failed only because they were never imagined clearly.
Students should remember that technical possibility alone guarantees adoption once a prototype exists.
200.explanation: The entry uses nuclear-powered cars, food pills, and 3D television to show that prediction needs reality checks about use, risk, cost, comfort, and adoption.
200.visibleCorrectExplanation: The entry uses nuclear-powered cars, food pills, and 3D television to show that prediction needs reality checks about use, risk, cost, comfort, and adoption.

visibleWrongExplanations.label: B
visibleWrongExplanations.answer: The entry mainly proves that every predicted technology eventually succeeds if people wait long enough.
visibleWrongExplanations.explanation: That reverses the point: some futures stay almost there because real-world adoption never catches up to the prediction.

visibleWrongExplanations.label: C
visibleWrongExplanations.answer: The key lesson is that nuclear cars, food pills, and 3D television failed only because they were never imagined clearly.
visibleWrongExplanations.explanation: The problem was not only imagination; the entry stresses practical limits such as safety, cost, usefulness, comfort, and adoption.

visibleWrongExplanations.label: D
visibleWrongExplanations.answer: Students should remember that technical possibility alone guarantees adoption once a prototype exists.
visibleWrongExplanations.explanation: The entry says the opposite: a technology can be technically possible and still fail if it is inconvenient, risky, costly, or not useful enough.

200.prompt: What more specific detail from "Things We Commit To but Do Not Finish" should a alpaca remember after the obvious fact?
200.correctAnswer: At the entry level, this helps students separate starting from finishing. At the section level, it gives the introduction a realistic counterpoint: some routes do not end in arrival. Key examples to keep available: The truth is that sometimes we’ll never get there at all; Most videogames are left unfinished; nearly half of those who start university don’t get a degree; Unfinished Video Games.
wrongAnswers: The entry says unfinished games and degrees mostly prove that people should never begin difficult routes.
The key detail is that modern technology always makes completion easier by removing distraction.
Students should remember that non-completion is always irrational failure rather than sometimes a changed cost-benefit decision.
200.explanation: The entry uses unfinished games, university degrees, and unfinished goals to show that starting and finishing are different problems shaped by time, support, pressure, distraction, and changing value.
200.visibleCorrectExplanation: The entry uses unfinished games, university degrees, and unfinished goals to show that starting and finishing are different problems shaped by time, support, pressure, distraction, and changing value.

visibleWrongExplanations.label: B
visibleWrongExplanations.answer: The entry says unfinished games and degrees mostly prove that people should never begin difficult routes.
visibleWrongExplanations.explanation: That overcorrects: the entry asks why commitments are not completed, not whether difficult routes should be avoided.

visibleWrongExplanations.label: C
visibleWrongExplanations.answer: The key detail is that modern technology always makes completion easier by removing distraction.
visibleWrongExplanations.explanation: The entry is more balanced: technology can help organize work, but it can also create new exits and distractions.

visibleWrongExplanations.label: D
visibleWrongExplanations.answer: Students should remember that non-completion is always irrational failure rather than sometimes a changed cost-benefit decision.
visibleWrongExplanations.explanation: The entry says not finishing can be failure, but it can also be a rational decision when the destination no longer justifies the cost.

300.prompt: Start with this detail: Some goals are destination-centered (finish line, graduation, arrival city). Which idea gives the best comparison?
wrongAnswers: False endings and near-endings
Detours
Point of no return
300.explanation: Correct: What counts as an ending.
300.visibleCorrectExplanation: Correct: What counts as an ending.
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Destination vs journey: Some goals are destination-centered (finish line, graduation, arrival city). It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. What counts as an ending is the best connection because it exposes ending as a decision point, not a natural fact; a rule, audience, institution, or feeling can each declare the finish at a different time. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that People love process talk, but schools, jobs, contests, and governments still reward visible endpoints. That tension drives the whole theme.

wrongAnswers: People always reject new inventions for no reason
Science stops working after the prototype stage
The most exciting idea is always the most practical
300.explanation: Correct: Safety, cost, usefulness, timing, or demand can block them.
300.visibleCorrectExplanation: Correct: Safety, cost, usefulness, timing, or demand can block them.

300.prompt: Why can modern technology make finishing harder?
300.correctAnswer: It can create distractions and endless alternative options
wrongAnswers: It always removes the need for self-control
It makes every long project simpler to complete
It prevents people from tracking progress
300.explanation: Correct: It can create distractions and endless alternative options.
